column1: # area: api | behavior | docs | examples | semantics | toolkit | performance | release

column3: Public constructor names, keyword arguments, state types, and event results are reviewed for compatibility across releases.
column4: Focused API docs, docstrings, and stable API baseline

column3: The widget is exported through Wicked.API and recorded in api/stable_api.tsv with the correct public binding kind.
Former experimental bindings have an explicit promote, qualify, or remove decision before any compatibility namespace export remains.
Rendering is deterministic across empty, normal, clipped, resized, narrow, and zero-size regions where applicable.
Behavior evidence points at a repository-relative checked-in Julia source file for the widget implementation.
Keyboard, pointer, focus, disabled, read-only, cancellation, and invalid-state behavior is tested or explicitly marked non-applicable.
Developer-facing docs explain construction, state ownership, event handling, styling hooks, layout behavior, and migration notes.
The widget appears in the component catalog, framework migration vocabulary, and family closeout material.
At least one public example is copyable and mapped to the owning widget family.
Interactive widgets expose semantic tree output, stable roles, labels, values, and state flags for testing and accessibility.
Toolkit integration works with keyed state, reactive updates, focus routing, CSS-like styling, and component composition where promised.
column4: Toolkit tests, API docs, and family evidence

column3: Normal construction, state creation, render, and event paths are covered by precompile workloads.
Large-data widgets document and test virtualization, paging, stale data, loading slots, and update costs.
column4: Data-display parity evidence and benchmark evidence

column3: The owning widget family has complete docs, examples, precompile evidence, zero blockers, and clean release metadata.
Release review is performed from complete coverage evidence with clean git provenance.
User-visible additions, deprecations, compatibility aliases, and removals are recorded.
